title: Materials Science and Solid Mechanics — SuperBEST v5 ELC Cost Analysis

superBEST_v5_costs.note: sqrt is a native op in v5 (cost 2n), cheaper than pow (cost 3n)

equations.name: Hooke's law

folded.strategy: No folding possible; E is a free material parameter, ε is a free variable

equations.notes: Linear law — same structure as Hubble's law (v = H₀d) and Fick's first law. Minimal cost for a two-terminal product.

equations.name: Young's modulus

folded.strategy: No folding; both σ and ε are free variables

equations.notes: Ratio form — minimal cost (1n). div is cheaper than mul in SuperBEST v5. Same cost as Wien's law (b/T).

equations.name: Poisson's ratio

folded.strategy: No folding; both strain components are free variables. neg is unavoidable.

equations.notes: The negative sign is definitional (Poisson's ratio is positive for most materials). neg is the dominant cost despite div being the structural op.

equations.name: Von Mises stress
equations.formula: σ_vm = √(σ₁² + σ₂² + σ₃² − σ₁σ₂ − σ₂σ₃ − σ₁σ₃)

folded.strategy: No constant folding possible — all six terms involve free variables σ₁,σ₂,σ₃. Structure is irreducible.
folded.breakdown: 27 (same as unfolded; no constants to absorb)

equations.notes: Von Mises is the most expensive equation in this set (27n). The three quadratic terms (pow×3=9n) and three cross-product multiplications (mul×3=6n) together account for 15n of 27n. sqrt in v5 costs 2n (native op), saving 1n vs. pow cost of 3n. Important for structural FEM solvers.

equations.name: Thermal expansion

folded.strategy: α is a material constant (cost 0); no further folding since L and ΔT are both free variables
folded.breakdown: 4 (same as unfolded; α already at cost 0 as terminal)

equations.notes: Two-multiplication chain. α is a material-specific terminal constant (cost 0). Same tree structure as any three-variable linear product.

equations.name: Creep rate (Norton-Arrhenius)

unfolded.breakdown: pow(3) + mul(2) + div(1) + neg(2) + exp(1) + mul(2) + mul(2) = 13

folded.strategy: A, R, Q are material/physical constants (cost 0). No structural fold possible since σ and T are free variables.
folded.breakdown: 13 (same as unfolded; A, R, Q already at cost 0 as terminals)

equations.dominant_op: pow (3n, single most expensive node); mul chain contributes 6n total
equations.notes: Compound structure: power law × Arrhenius. The Arrhenius branch (mul+div+neg+exp = 6n) and the power law branch (pow = 3n) are largely independent subtrees joined by mul. This is the most structurally complex equation in the set (two distinct physical mechanisms composed multiplicatively).

equations.name: Fracture toughness (stress intensity factor)

folded.strategy: π is a numeric constant (cost 0). If a (crack half-length) is fixed, fold √(πa) as a constant → only mul(σ, const) = 2n. Otherwise no fold.
tree_standard: √π as const (π is terminal, cost 0); a is free
folded.breakdown_sqrt_pi_fold: Note: √(πa) ≠ √π·√a structurally in ELC unless explicitly split. Primary form: mul(π,a) then sqrt, total 6n.
folded.breakdown_a_fixed: If a is a fixed geometric constant, fold √(πa) → 2n (just mul(σ,const))

equations.dominant_op: mul chain (three mul/sqrt nodes at equal cost 2n each)
equations.notes: sqrt costs 2n in SuperBEST v5 (native op). mul(π,a) = 2n because π is terminal (cost 0) but a is a free variable. Three equal-cost nodes at 2n each.

equations.name: Fatigue life (Basquin's law)

folded.strategy: C is a material constant (cost 0). b is a material exponent (cost 0 as terminal). No structural folding possible.

equations.notes: Power law form — same tree shape as A·xⁿ. b is the Basquin exponent (typically −0.05 to −0.12 for metals). pow dominates.

equations.name: Fourier heat conduction (and Fick's diffusion isomorphism)

folded.strategy: k (thermal conductivity) is a material constant (cost 0). dT/dx is a single input terminal (given). No folding possible.

equations.dominant_op: mul and neg at equal cost (2n each)

isomorphism.explanation: Fourier and Fick share the identical ELC tree structure. Both are linear flux laws: flux = −(transport_coefficient)·(driving_gradient). Same cost (4n), same depth, same operator sequence. This is a cross-domain structural isomorphism.

equations.notes: neg is unavoidable (the minus sign is physically meaningful — flux opposes gradient). If orientation is absorbed into sign of k/D by convention, cost drops to 2n (just mul). Reported as 4n (sign explicit).

equations.name: Clausius-Mossotti relation

tree: mul(3, ε₀)          → cost 2  [3 and ε₀ are terminals, cost 0 each]

folded.strategy: RHS: fold 3ε₀ as single constant C₀ (cost 0) → div(mul(N,α), C₀) = mul(N,α)·recip(C₀) or equivalently mul(N, mul(α, recip(C₀))). With recip: 2+1+2=5n. Alternative: fold 1/(3ε₀) as const → mul(N, mul(α, const)) = 2+2=4n. LHS cannot fold; ε is the free variable.

equations.dominant_op: sub and add (equal cost 2n, structural symmetry on LHS)
equations.notes: Perfect cost symmetry: both sides evaluate to 5n unfolded. LHS is an affine transformation of ε; RHS is a ratio of products. RHS allows minor fold (3ε₀ as constant). The equation expresses a deep physical symmetry (polarizability ↔ dielectric response) and it is reflected in the ELC cost symmetry.

summary_table.notes: Minimal linear law — same tree as Hubble's law
Most expensive in set; no constants to fold; sqrt=2n in v5 (saves 1n vs pow)
Compound: power law × Arrhenius; two independent mechanism subtrees
sqrt=2n (v5 native); mul(π,a) required since a is free
Isomorphic trees — same ELC cost (4n); cross-domain structural identity
5n each side — perfect cost symmetry; RHS folds to 4n with 3ε₀ as constant

aggregate.folding_note: Almost no constants to fold in materials equations — variables dominate all expressions

key_findings.detail: Breakdown: 3 pow×3n + 2 add×2n + 3 mul×2n + 3 sub×2n + 1 sqrt×2n = 9+4+6+6+2 = 27n. No constant folding possible — all six input terms are free variables. Most expensive single equation in continuum mechanics surveyed so far.

Fourier heat conduction (q = −k·∇T) and Fick's first law (J = −D·∇C) share the identical ELC tree: neg(mul(transport_coefficient, gradient)). Same operator sequence, same depth, same cost. This is a genuine cross-domain structural isomorphism — the ELC tree is transport-law-agnostic.

The creep rate equation decomposes into two independent mechanism subtrees: power law branch (pow(σ,n) = 3n) and Arrhenius branch (mul+div+neg+exp = 6n), joined by a final mul chain (2+2 = 4n). Total 13n. No other equation in this set has this two-mechanism compound structure.

key_findings.finding: All materials equations are inside ELC(ℝ)
key_findings.detail: All 10 equations from continuum mechanics and solid mechanics — including stress, strain, fracture, fatigue, thermal, creep, and polarization — are expressible using only {mul, div, pow, exp, ln, neg, add, sub, sqrt}. No trigonometric or inverse-trig functions appear. This confirms that classical continuum mechanics is fully contained within ELC(ℝ).

Both sides of the Clausius-Mossotti relation independently evaluate to 5n unfolded. This reflects the structural balance of the physical law: an affine transformation of ε (LHS) is matched against a polarizability-density ratio (RHS), both of identical operator complexity.
In v5, sqrt is a primitive at cost 2n rather than derived from pow (which costs 3n). This affects Von Mises (saves 1n) and Fracture toughness (saves 1n) vs. a v3/v4 analysis.
